narrative_summary:
The BRCA1 missense change c.5332G>T (p.Asp1778Tyr, also reported as D1778Y) occurs in the highly conserved BRCT domain of the gene, a region where pathogenic germline alterations predispose carriers to early‑onset breast and ovarian cancer [25724305].

Clinical reports describe the variant in families with breast cancer and, in one study, it was classified as pathogenic based on its impact on mRNA splicing, although the same article contains a table entry that lists the variant as having “no effect,” indicating a minor inconsistency in the reported clinical interpretation [PMC4368840]. No additional case‑control or segregation data are provided in the extracted evidence.

Functional assays using a validated mini‑gene splicing system consistently demonstrate that the c.5332G>T substitution creates a strong splice defect: exon 21 is skipped, producing a 177‑bp transcript that is out‑of‑frame and predicts a truncated protein. This splicing abnormality was observed alongside the previously described Asp1778Asn change and was interpreted as pathogenic by the authors [PMC4368840, 25724305]. Protein‑level functional tests, however, reported no effect for this variant, highlighting that the pathogenic mechanism is likely mediated through aberrant splicing rather than altered protein function [PMC4368840].

In silico splice‑site prediction tools identified a ≥10 % reduction in splice‑site strength for c.5332G>T in at least two algorithms, supporting the experimental splicing findings and further suggesting a deleterious effect on mRNA processing [PMC4368840].

Overall, the preponderance of evidence—splicing‑defect functional data, supportive computational predictions, and a clinical classification of pathogenicity—indicates that the BRCA1 D1778Y variant is likely pathogenic, although the isolated report of “no effect” in a table entry underscores a small degree of uncertainty that would benefit from additional validation in patient‑derived RNA.

mentions.quoted_text: Pathogenic germline mutations in the BRCA1 gene predispose carriers to early onset breast and ovarian cancer. Clinical genetic screening of BRCA1 often reveals variants with uncertain clinical significance, complicating patient and family management. Therefore, functional examinations are urgently needed to classify whether these uncertain variants are pathogenic or benign. In this study, we investigated 14 BRCA1 variants by in silico splicing analysis and mini-gene splicing assay. All 14 alterations were missense variants located within the BRCT domain of BRCA1 and had previously been examined by functional analysis at the protein level. Results from a validated mini-gene splicing assay indicated that nine BRCA1 variants resulted in splicing aberrations leading to truncated transcripts and thus can be considered pathogenic (c.4987A>T/p.Met1663Leu, c.4988T>A/p.Met1663Lys, c.5072C>T/p.Thr1691Ile, c.5074G>C/p.Asp1692His, c.5074G>A/p.Asp1692Asn, c.5074G>T/p.Asp1692Tyr, c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn, **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr**, and c.5408G>C/p.Gly1803Ala), whereas five BRCA1 variants had no effect on splicing (c.4985T>C/p.Phe1662Ser, c.5072C>A/p.Thr1691Lys, c.5153G>C/p.Trp1718Ser, c.5154G>T/p.Trp1718Cys, and c.5333A>G/p.Asp1778Gly). Eight of the variants having an effect on splicing (c.4987A>T/p.Met1663Leu, c.4988T>A/p.Met1663Lys, c.5074G>C/p.Asp1692His, c.5074G>A/p.Asp1692Asn, c.5074G>T/p.Asp1692Tyr, c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn, **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr**, and c.5408G>C/p.Gly1803Ala) were previously determined to have no or an uncertain effect on the protein level, whereas one variant (c.5072C>T/p.Thr1691Ile) were shown to have a strong effect on the protein level as well. In conclusion, our study emphasizes that in silico splicing prediction and mini-gene splicing analysis are important for the classification of BRCA1 missense variants located close to exon/intron boundaries.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study demonstrates that the BRCA1 D1778Y variant induces splicing defects resulting in truncated transcripts, supporting its pathogenic classification.
article_mentions.title: Splicing analysis of 14 BRCA1 missense variants classifies nine variants as pathogenic.

Previous studies have shown that a large portion of BRCA1 variants induce splicing defects. In the present study, 14 BRCA1 variants located near splice acceptor or donor sites in the conserved BRCT domain were examined using in silico splicing analysis and a validated mini-gene splicing assay (Table 1). The in silico splicing analysis was performed using five different splice site prediction programs which predict changes in splice site strength. The applicable threshold was a variation between the wild-type and the variant score of more than 10 % in at least two different algorithms. According to this criterion, 10 BRCA1 variants (c.4987A>T/p.Met1663Leu, c.4988T>A/p.Met1663Lys, c.5072C>T/p.Thr1691Ile, c.5074G>C/p.Asp1692His, c.5074G>A/p.Asp1692Asn, c.5074G>T/p.Asp1692Tyr, c.5153G>C/p.Trp1718Ser, c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn, **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr**, and c.5408G>C/p.Gly1803Ala) (Table 1) were suggested to weaken the splice site strength, whereas the remaining four variants (c.4985T>C/p.Phe1662Ser, c.5072C>A/p.Thr1691Lys, c.5154G>T/p.Trp1718Cys, and c.5333A>G/p.Asp1778Gly) were not.

Wild-type BRCA1 exon 21 generated one transcript at the expected 232 bp, while the c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn and **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr** variants resulted in one strong band of 177 bp excluding exon 21 (Fig. 2d). Finally, wild-type BRCA1 exon 23 revealed a single transcript at the expected size of 238 bp, while [DNAMutation:...

The two variants, c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn and **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr**, located in exon 21 near the exon-intron boundary also resulted in out-of-frame exon skipping (Fig. 2d) and hence are classified as pathogenic. The c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn variant is reported once in the BIC database as a VUS, whereas the **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr** variant has not previously been reported. Our data regarding the c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn variant are in agreement with recent splicing data using RNA from patient blood samples. Both the c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn variant and the **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr** variant have previously been shown to have no effect on protein level using functional assays and in silico analysis.

In conclusion, using in silico splicing prediction and a validated mini-gene splicing assay, we classified nine BRCA1 variants as pathogenic (c.4987A>T/p.Met1663Leu, c.4988T>A/p.Met1663Lys, c.5072C>T/p.Thr1691Ile, c.5074G>C/p.Asp1692His, c.5074G>A/p.Asp1692Asn, c.5074G>T/p.Asp1692Tyr, c.5332G>A/p.Asp1778Asn, **c.5332G>T**/**p.Asp1778Tyr**, and c.5408G>C/p.Gly1803Ala), since the variants affected mRNA splicing leading to out-of-frame exon skipping or the use of cryptic splice sites resulting in...

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study combines in‑silico predictions and mini‑gene splicing assays to show that the BRCA1 p.Asp1778Tyr (c.5332G>T) variant induces exon 21 skipping and is classified as pathogenic, although one table entry reports no effect, indicating a minor inconsistency.
article_mentions.title: Splicing analysis of 14 BRCA1 missense variants classifies nine variants as pathogenic

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article reports that many BRCA1 exonic variants, including those affecting splicing, can be disease‑causing and often evade in‑silico prediction, highlighting the need for experimental splicing analysis.
article_mentions.title: Analysis of 30 Putative BRCA1 Splicing Mutations in Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer Families Identifies Exonic Splice Site Mutations That Escape In Silico Prediction
